name: Carbone
editorial: The Major Food Group's Italian-American time machine. Tableside Caesars, lobster fra diavolo, a veal parm the size of a hubcap. Reservations drop 30 days out at 10am ET on Resy and evaporate in seconds. A reservation here is social currency in NYC.

name: Don Angie
editorial: Angie Rito and Scott Tacinelli's tiny West Village Italian, with a mirrored ceiling and the lasagna pinwheel that broke Instagram. Two-tops are the hardest get; group tables open more often. Repeat customers swear by the chrysanthemum salad.

name: Tatiana by Kwame Onwuachi
editorial: Kwame Onwuachi's love letter to the Bronx, perched at Lincoln Center. The NYT's 2023 Restaurant of the Year. Curry goat patties, oxtail dumplings, braised short rib pastrami. Big tables go faster than two-tops because of the pre-show theater crowd.

name: 4 Charles Prime Rib
editorial: Brendan Sodikoff's clubby West Village steakhouse. The signature prime rib, the 4 Charles burger at the bar, and a wine list that rewards spending. Books out 30 days in advance the second the calendar opens. Late slots (9:30+) and bar seating are the realistic gets.

name: The Polo Bar
editorial: Ralph Lauren's flagship restaurant: equestrian leather, brass tack lighting, a power-lunch crowd in cashmere. Famously hard to book without a connection. Lunch is slightly easier than dinner. Dress code enforced.

name: Rao's
editorial: Tables are owned by regulars, on the same night each year. New reservations are functionally impossible — but cancellations do occur. The Sinatra-era room, the lemon chicken, the meatballs. A bucket-list restaurant for serious eaters.

name: Le Bernardin
editorial: Eric Ripert's three-Michelin-starred temple to seafood. A four-decade Times four-star. Refined, hushed, with service that anticipates needs. Lunch is a comparative bargain. Books 30 days in advance; window-table requests rarely honored.

name: Eleven Madison Park
editorial: Daniel Humm's reinvented plant-based tasting menu in the soaring Madison Square Park dining room. Three Michelin stars, often debated, never ignored. Bar room is the easier in-road and serves a more flexible menu.

name: Sushi Nakazawa
editorial: Daisuke Nakazawa, of Jiro Dreams of Sushi, runs the bar in a tight West Village space. The omakase is the order. Counter seats are the experience; tables are not. Two-tops at the counter are the hardest get.

name: I Sodi
editorial: Rita Sodi's candlelit Tuscan jewel box, expanded to a slightly larger room in 2022 but still nearly unbookable. The 12-layer lasagna and the negroni on tap. Couples plan trips around it.

name: Rezdôra
editorial: Stefano Secchi's Emilia-Romagna pasta workshop. The Tour of Emilia tasting (seven pastas) is the move. Resy drops are 28 days out, 12pm ET. A perennial Top 10 list pick.

name: Lucali
editorial: Mark Iacono's candlelit Carroll Gardens pizzeria. Walk-in only, with a list system that requires arriving when the doors open at 5pm. The thin-crust pie is the ur-Brooklyn-pie. BYOB.

name: L'Artusi
editorial: The West Village's reliable hit: roasted broccoli with chili and pecorino, ricotta gnocchi, garganelli with bolognese. Two floors absorb the demand but prime times still book a month out.

name: Lilia
editorial: Missy Robbins' converted-auto-body-shop pasta haven. The mafaldine with pink peppercorn, sheep's milk cheese ravioli. Drops 30 days out; Williamsburg's hardest table.

name: Misi
editorial: Missy Robbins' second act: a glass-walled pasta theater on the Williamsburg waterfront. Vegetable-forward antipasti, simple-looking pastas with deep technique. Bar seats are the most flexible option.

name: Atomix
vibe: tasting-menu counter, design-forward, two Michelin stars
editorial: Junghyun Park's two-Michelin-starred Korean tasting menu in a hidden NoMad townhouse. 14 courses, course cards designed by his wife Ellia. Books out 60 days in advance. The hardest counter in NYC.

name: Cote
editorial: The Michelin-starred Korean steakhouse blueprint: dry-aged USDA Prime, banchan that keep coming, soju cocktails. The Butcher's Feast is the classic order. Walk-in bar seats are realistic; reservations are not.

name: Estela
editorial: Ignacio Mattos's upstairs Houston Street hideaway: ricotta dumplings in burnt butter, beef tartare with sunchokes, the lamb-ribs-with-honey that everyone orders. The bar serves the full menu and is the path of least resistance.

name: Balthazar
editorial: Keith McNally's enduring SoHo brasserie. The brunch is a tourist tradition; the late-night menu is for industry. Big tables open the most; weekend brunch the hardest.

name: Pastis
editorial: McNally's Meatpacking comeback. Steak frites, oysters, the scene. Brunch is mob-deep; weeknight dinners around 6:30 are the underrated slot.

name: Frenchette
editorial: Riad Nasr and Lee Hanson's reborn Tribeca bistro: rotisserie duck for two, natural wine, a chef-y crowd. The bar serves the full menu. Two Michelin stars as of 2024.

name: The Modern
editorial: Two-Michelin-starred Danny Meyer flagship overlooking the MoMA sculpture garden. Pre-museum lunch is the move. The bar room is more flexible; the dining room is for occasions.

name: Per Se
editorial: Thomas Keller's New York French Laundry. Three Michelin stars, $390+ tasting menu, salmon cornets and oysters and pearls. Dinner is the special occasion; salon menu in the lounge is the discovery hack.

name: Daniel
editorial: Daniel Boulud's UES temple. Two Michelin stars, tasting menus, a bar/lounge that punches above its weight. A good business-anniversary spot.

name: Torrisi
editorial: MFG's Puck Building flagship, a love letter to NY-Italian dining. Sticky ribs, scampi, the Sunday gravy. Drops with Carbone-level intensity.

name: Kjun
editorial: Jae Lee's 12-seat counter, where Korean staples meet Louisiana technique. One Michelin star. Blackened gulf shrimp with kimchi. Booked the second tickets drop on Tock.

name: Via Carota
editorial: The Sodi/Williams sister-restaurant to I Sodi. As of 2023, takes reservations on Resy at 9am, 14 days out. The svizzerina (Tuscan-style burger) and insalata verde are the move.

name: Saint Julivert Fisherie
editorial: Alex Raij and Eder Montero's Cobble Hill seafood snug. Mexican prawn aguachile, a singular fideos with squid. Tiny room — the demand is mostly local.

name: Lodi
editorial: Ignacio Mattos's Milan-inspired all-day café in Rockefeller Center. Aperitivo hour with cicchetti is the underrated way in. Lunch crowds skew midtown professional.

name: Shukette
editorial: Ayesha Nurdjaja's Chelsea hummus temple. The kataifi-wrapped halloumi and the laffa with everything. Group-friendly, prime-time is the squeeze.
